user_task: Standalone Audio extraction (MP3 / WAV) - Transcribe audio to text chunks; produces time-aligned segments. English-only.
source: JTBD & user tasks tab, EXTRACT bucket — 'Standalone Audio extraction (MP3 / WAV)'; seed queries from 'NeMo Retriever — JTBD seed queries' tab, EXTRACT → audio transcription.

success_criteria.quality: operational pass — (1) audio extras + ffmpeg present; (2) ASR (nvidia/parakeet-ctc-1.1b local HF, or a remote Parakeet/Riva gRPC endpoint) loads; (3) ingest of a .wav/.mp3 returns NON-EMPTY time-aligned transcript segments — each row carries source_id (the audio path) + per-segment timing (segment_start_seconds/segment_end_seconds, type=audio) and the segment text; (4) the transcript is queryable. Binary pass/fail. NON-ENGLISH guard: Parakeet-CTC-1.1b is English-only and the library has NO ASR language flag — on non-English audio it silently emits garbage, so the SKILL layer must detect a non-English filename/locale hint and warn rather than transcribe blindly.
success_criteria.time: medium — single-clip transcription <= 2 min (cold ASR load + a short clip).
success_criteria.trigger_rate: >= 95% — a 'transcribe this audio / make my audio searchable' prompt must fire the skill.
success_criteria.subcommand_accuracy: >= 90% — must run `retriever ingest <audio>` (format auto-detected from .wav/.mp3; NO --input-type) then `retriever query`; segmentation rungs use --segment-audio / --audio-split-type / --audio-split-interval.

environment.install: pip/uv install '.[multimedia]' PLUS the ffmpeg host package (apt/brew). Without either, audio ingest fails. See skills/nemo-retriever/references/setup.md ('Audio / video').
environment.asr_model: nvidia/parakeet-ctc-1.1b — loads locally via the [multimedia] extra (HF), OR a remote Parakeet/Riva gRPC endpoint (dry-run plan: asr.audio_infer_protocol=grpc, asr.audio_endpoints, asr.segment_audio). English-only; there is NO ASR language flag (--ocr-lang only selects the OCR-v2 language for document images, not ASR).
environment.auto_detect: `retriever ingest <path>` auto-detects audio from the .mp3 / .wav (also .m4a) extension; .flac is silently filtered (unsupported audio format). There is NO --input-type flag on the shipped CLI.
environment.audio_metadata_layer: Per-row audio timing is exposed in the LanceDB row metadata, NOT in the query-result keys. A query hit has exactly {source, page_number, text}. Segment timing is verified at the extract/metadata layer: each row's metadata JSON carries {type: 'audio', segment_start_seconds, segment_end_seconds}; the row's source field carries {source_id, source_name} = the audio path. (Library schema names these content_metadata.start_time / end_time in milliseconds; the LanceDB CLI row surfaces them as segment_start_seconds / segment_end_seconds in seconds.)
environment.fixtures_note: Only a WAV fixture exists in the catalog (data/multimodal_test.wav, ~16 s, 16 kHz). The MP3 path is IDENTICAL (auto-detected by extension, same ASR stage) — to exercise MP3 specifically a .mp3 fixture would need to be added; here .wav stands in. Non-English cases reuse the same WAV bytes but with a non-English-LOCALE filename so the guard fires on the name/locale hint (the library has no language detector).
environment.grounding_note: All expected outputs below were captured from a REAL local ingest+query of data/multimodal_test.wav in this venv (segment-audio, time split, interval 5): `Ingested 1 file(s) -> 6 row(s)`; 6 monotonically time-aligned audio segments; transcript text 'Section one.' / 'This is the first section of the document.' / ... ; query returned {source, page_number, text} citing the .wav.

cases.title: Single WAV -> non-empty transcript rows
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the core EXTRACT task at its simplest: hand one .wav to `retriever ingest` (audio auto-detected) and get back NON-EMPTY transcript rows. Adds nothing yet — it is the baseline that ASR loads and produces text.
cases.prompt: Transcribe data/meeting_clip.wav and tell me what the speaker is discussing.

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 1 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (branch_summary audio:1)
expected_output.transcript: Non-empty transcript segments. Against the WAV fixture: 'Section one.', 'This is the first section of the document.', 'It has some more place', 'Text to show how the document looks like.', 'The text is not meant to be', 'Meaningful or informative, but rather to demonstrate the layout and formatting of the document.'
expected_output.query_shape: JSON array of hits; each hit {source, page_number, text}; >= 1 hit whose source is the .wav path
expected_output.answer: Summarize from the returned transcript segments (do not fabricate); cite source=meeting_clip.wav

pass_when: audio extras + ffmpeg present and ASR (parakeet-ctc-1.1b or remote gRPC) loads
single `retriever ingest data/meeting_clip.wav` auto-detects audio (branch_summary audio:1) and writes >= 1 row
transcript rows are NON-EMPTY and query returns >= 1 hit citing the .wav

cases.title: Verify time-aligned segments (start/end timing in row metadata)
cases.complexity_axis: Adds the 'produces time-aligned segments' clause of the task: not just that text comes back, but that each segment row carries per-segment timing. Over the baseline it inspects the row metadata to assert segment_start_seconds / segment_end_seconds + type=audio + source_id are present and increasing.
cases.prompt: Get the transcript out of data/customer_call_001.wav, and for each line show me its start and end time in the recording so I can jump to it.

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 1 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (branch_summary audio:1)
expected_output.timing_assertion: Each transcript row's metadata JSON carries {type: 'audio', segment_start_seconds, segment_end_seconds}; timings are non-negative and monotonically increasing across segments. Row source carries {source_id, source_name} = customer_call_001.wav. (Library schema: content_metadata.start_time/end_time in ms.)
expected_output.observed_example: On the WAV fixture, 6 segments e.g. start=0.48/end=0.88 'Section one.'; start=1.12/end=2.56 'This is the first section...'; ... ; start=10.35/end=14.75 'Meaningful or informative...'.
expected_output.answer: Transcript lines each annotated with their start_time/end_time, citing customer_call_001.wav

pass_when: ingest produces multiple time-aligned segments (not one blob)
each row carries segment_start_seconds + segment_end_seconds (type=audio) in metadata and source_id = the audio path
segment timings are non-negative and non-decreasing across rows

cases.title: Segmentation control (--segment-audio + --audio-split-type/-interval)
cases.complexity_axis: Adds explicit control over HOW the clip is chunked. Over rung 2 (which only observed default segments) this drives --audio-split-type time --audio-split-interval to re-chunk the SAME clip and verifies the segment count / boundaries change with the interval. Exercises the task's segmentation flags directly.
cases.prompt: I have a longer recording data/long_briefing.wav. Transcribe it but split it into roughly 5-second time chunks so the segments are short and easy to cite.

dry_run_plan_assertions: audio.enabled == true AND audio.split_type == 'time' AND audio.split_interval == 5
expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 1 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (observed: 6 rows for the ~16 s WAV at 5 s time-split)
expected_output.segmentation_assertion: Segments respect the chosen split type/interval; changing --audio-split-interval changes the segment count / boundaries vs. the default. Each segment still carries segment_start_seconds/segment_end_seconds.
expected_output.answer: Transcript returned as short time-bounded chunks, citing long_briefing.wav

pass_when: --segment-audio + --audio-split-type time + --audio-split-interval are passed and reflected in the resolved plan (audio.split_type/split_interval, asr.segment_audio)
ingest yields multiple short time-aligned segments consistent with the interval
split-type value is one of size|time|frame (not an invalid value)

cases.title: Folder of audio -> searchable; keyword query finds a mention
cases.complexity_axis: Adds folder-level ingest + retrieval intent. Over rung 3 (one clip) it ingests a WHOLE folder of audio in one invocation (branch_summary audio:3) into one index, then proves the corpus is SEARCHABLE: a keyword query returns the segment(s) mentioning it, with the audio file + timing as the citation.
cases.prompt: Make my folder of podcast recordings in data/ searchable so I can find any mention of a given topic. Load them all, then find where a 'section' is mentioned.

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 3 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (branch_summary audio:3)
expected_output.search_assertion: Query returns >= 1 hit whose text contains the keyword (e.g. 'Section one.' / 'This is the first section of the document.'); the hit's source is one of the podcast .wav files.
expected_output.answer: Point the user to the matching episode(s)/segment(s) for the keyword, citing the .wav source

pass_when: a SINGLE `retriever ingest data/` ingests all 3 audio files (no per-file loop; branch_summary audio:3)
query over the resulting index returns a hit containing the keyword, citing one of the audio files
the corpus is searchable end-to-end (ingest -> query round-trip on audio)

cases.title: Acceptance: English clip transcribes + queryable with timing citation, AND non-English-named clip triggers the English-only guard
cases.complexity_axis: Composed operational-pass gate for the row. Over the prior rungs it requires BOTH halves of the task at once: (a) the English happy path — transcribe + segment + query a clip with timing citations into a named index; AND (b) the negative guard — a clip whose name/locale signals non-English must NOT be transcribed blindly; the skill must WARN (English-only) instead, since the library has no language detector and would emit garbage. This ties directly to negative test (d).
cases.prompt: Two recordings in data/: town_hall_en.wav (English) and reunion_fr-FR.wav (French). Transcribe the English one into an index called 'audio_smoke', confirm I can search it and that each result carries its timestamp in the recording. For the French one, do the right thing.
expected_commands: # data/reunion_fr-FR.wav: NOT ingested blindly — skill warns it is English-only (Parakeet-CTC-1.1b, no ASR language flag); non-English would yield garbage transcript

english_path.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 1 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/audio_smoke. (branch_summary audio:1)
english_path.timing_assertion: Each returned/stored segment carries segment_start_seconds + segment_end_seconds (type=audio) and source_id = town_hall_en.wav
english_path.query_alignment: query reads the SAME table (audio_smoke) the ingest wrote; >= 1 hit citing town_hall_en.wav with a timestamp
english_path.answer: Answer grounded in the English transcript, with a time-aligned citation (segment start/end) to town_hall_en.wav

expected_output.non_english_guard: The skill detects the non-English locale hint in the filename ('fr-FR') and WARNS that audio ASR is English-only (parakeet-ctc-1.1b; no language flag) rather than transcribing reunion_fr-FR.wav and returning garbage. Either it skips the French file with a clear warning, or it surfaces the limitation and asks the user to confirm.

pass_when: English: ingest -> audio_smoke and query reads audio_smoke (table names aligned); answer cites town_hall_en.wav with segment timing
every returned hit carries non-null source + time-aligned segment metadata (start/end seconds)
Non-English guard fires: reunion_fr-FR.wav is NOT silently transcribed; the skill warns it is English-only (ties to negative test d)
end-to-end transcription of the English clip <= 2 min
cases.known_caveat: The library has NO ASR language detector and NO language flag for ASR (--ocr-lang affects only OCR of document images). On non-English audio Parakeet-CTC-1.1b silently emits garbage English-ish text. Detecting non-English is therefore the SKILL layer's responsibility, keyed off filename/locale hints (e.g. 'fr-FR'); there is no in-library signal to assert against. If the skill omits this guard, the French file 'passes' ingest but produces meaningless rows — exactly the known failure mode this rung gates.
